name: OC Housing Now
tagline: The state said build. We're making sure it happens.
mission:
California's state housing mandates — SB 9, SB 10, RHNA, Builder's Remedy — provide the legal framework for the housing OC needs. The problem isn't bad law. The problem is local governments actively evading compliance.

The City of Orange had its Housing Element REJECTED by the California Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) in 2023. That rejection triggered Builder's Remedy — developers can now bypass local zoning for projects that include affordable units. Multiple applications are pending, including the Orange Heights 1,180-unit development. But the city is fighting every one of them.

87.5% of Orange County cities made their SB 9 ordinances MORE restrictive than state law requires. This is systematic noncompliance dressed up as local control.

This is not Austin's YIMBY fight. In Austin, you push local government to ADOPT pro-housing policy. In Orange County, you push local government to COMPLY WITH state housing mandates it's actively trying to evade. The legal tools already exist. What's missing is organized pressure to use them.

OC Housing Now exists because the enforcement gap between state mandate and local compliance is where housing dies. We track RHNA compliance across all 35 OC cities. We support Builder's Remedy applications. We show up at city councils when Housing Elements are on the agenda. We make the political cost of noncompliance higher than the cost of building.

The Irvine General Plan update proposes zoning for 57,656 new units — one of the largest zoning capacity increases in Southern California history. The Irvine Company (Donald Bren, America's richest real estate developer) controls ~75% of Irvine's apartment units. Whether the high-density scenario survives depends on organized pro-housing advocacy. We fight for maximum housing capacity because every unit not zoned is a family priced out.
theory_of_change: California's state housing mandates provide the legal framework. The bottleneck is local compliance. Organized advocates showing up at city councils, supporting Builder's Remedy applications, tracking RHNA compliance numbers across all 35 OC cities, and filing complaints with HCD when cities resist creates pressure for compliance. The legal leverage already exists — we activate it. In a purple county where council seats are decided by hundreds of votes, elected officials who block housing face electoral consequences when their noncompliance is made visible. We make it visible.

political_positioning.description: Pragmatic, broad-tent pro-housing coalition. Slightly right of center economically — we lead with property rights and market-based housing supply arguments, which resonate in OC's more conservative political environment. Socially moderate. The coalition includes YIMBY urbanists, property rights advocates, developers frustrated by local obstruction, renters squeezed by artificial scarcity, and state compliance advocates who believe the law should be followed. Similar to Austin YIMBY Action but shifted slightly right because OC's political center is further right than Austin's. Not affiliated with any party — we work with anyone who wants to build housing.

key_policy_positions: Enforce RHNA compliance across all 35 Orange County cities — track and publicize compliance status
Support Builder's Remedy applications in non-compliant cities including Orange
Oppose restrictive local SB 9 ordinances that undermine state housing law
Advocate for the high-density scenario in Irvine's General Plan update (57,656 units)
Push the City of Orange to adopt a compliant Housing Element and stop fighting state mandates
Support transit-oriented development along Metrolink corridors and the new OC Streetcar line
Eliminate or reduce parking minimums in OC cities to lower housing construction costs
File HCD complaints when cities adopt policies that obstruct state housing mandates
Support the Orange Heights 1,180-unit Builder's Remedy development
Advocate for ADU-friendly policies that go beyond minimum SB 9 requirements

active_campaigns.title: City of Orange Housing Element Accountability
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): The fight has shifted. The City of Orange appears to have achieved Housing Element compliance — it is NOT on Governor Newsom's March 25 noncompliant list (92% of CA communities now compliant). The city re-adopted its Housing Element in October 2023.

However, the Builder's Remedy legacy is where the real battle continues. Four Builder's Remedy applications filed during the noncompliance period total 696 units: Integral Communities (209 townhomes behind Village of Orange mall), Stonefield Development (138 apartments at Chapman-Yorba), and two Milan Capital projects (118 townhomes at Mara Brandman Horse Arena, 231 units at Sully-Miller quarry). The city has been fighting these — requiring zoning changes and general plan amendments despite Builder's Remedy, tactics struck down by LA County judges in similar cases.

AB 1893 (effective 2025) formalized Builder's Remedy as an entitlement pathway and prevents cities from requiring extra approvals — strengthening developers' legal position. Additionally, Californians for Homeownership sued the city alleging the approved Housing Element included parcels with deed restrictions forbidding housing.

The campaign shifts from 'force compliance' to 'ensure compliance produces actual housing.' Monitor whether the city processes the 696 pending Builder's Remedy units in good faith. The November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) will determine whether Orange's housing posture improves or regresses.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Compliance without implementation is performative. The city achieved technical compliance, but if it continues to obstruct the 696 Builder's Remedy units, the compliance is hollow. AB 1893 gives developers stronger legal tools, but legal rights without organized local support lead to protracted fights. Our role shifts from forcing compliance to ensuring the compliance produces actual housing. Public tracking of Builder's Remedy application processing, attendance at council hearings, and coordination with California YIMBY on enforcement keep pressure on the city to act in good faith.
next_actions: Track status of all four pending Builder's Remedy applications (696 total units) through the approval process
Monitor whether the city complies with AB 1893 by processing applications without requiring extra zoning approvals
Attend every council meeting where Builder's Remedy projects are on the agenda
Track Californians for Homeownership lawsuit progress — their challenge strengthens the enforcement framework
Coordinate with California YIMBY on AB 1893 enforcement and state-level compliance pressure
Prepare for November 2026 council elections — evaluate candidates on housing and Builder's Remedy positions
Publicize Newsom's March 25 enforcement escalation (15 noncompliant cities, AG referrals) as context for Orange's obligations

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of City of Orange Housing Element Accountability often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If City of Orange Housing Element Accountability is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move City of Orange Housing Element Accountability forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Irvine General Plan — Implementation & Irvine Company Monitoring
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 19, 2026): ⚡ MAJOR MILESTONE — Irvine City Council APPROVED the zoning text amendment on April 14, enabling the 'Alternative Open Space Approach' for Planning Area 12 (Oak Creek). This means the 50-acre nature park can now legally substitute for the golf course as open space. This was the critical procedural step — broader project approvals for the 3,100-unit development remain pending.

Previously: Planning Commission voted UNANIMOUSLY (7-0) on March 19 to recommend the zoning change.

The Irvine Company's revised package includes: $96 million in community benefit fees, 2,000 rent vouchers ($72 million value), land donations, a 50-acre nature park creating a trail corridor from Limestone Canyon through central Irvine to Quail Hill, and a new elementary school within the 3,100-unit residential village (1,500 single-family homes + 1,600 apartments on the 193-acre former Oak Creek Golf Course).

Opposition remains active: Former mayors Sally Anne Sheridan and Christina Shea are gathering signatures (~19,000 needed) for a citizen's initiative. The Woodbridge Village Association board formally opposes the conversion. Council could still decide to put the broader project on the November 2026 ballot instead of approving directly.

TRANSIT CONNECTION: The OC Streetcar ($649M, 4.15 miles, Santa Ana to Garden Grove) launch target revised to AUGUST 2026. In active daytime street testing since April 8 — vehicles running on city streets during regular hours. OC's first modern rail transit line is in final testing phase.

The General Plan (ADOPTED August 13, 2024) with 57,656 units is in implementation phase. The November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) remain critical — if the 5-2 Democratic majority shifts, implementation could be slowed.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The General Plan adoption was the policy win. Now the risk is implementation failure — the plan zones for 57,656 units but private developers must actually build them. The Irvine Company's 3,100-unit Oak Creek proposal is the bellwether: if it proceeds smoothly, it signals the plan is translating into housing. If it stalls, the plan is paper. Supporting pro-housing council candidates in November 2026 protects the implementation trajectory. Monitoring the Irvine Company's pricing and project mix ensures the monopoly holder doesn't capture all the value while constraining supply.
next_actions: Track Irvine Company Oak Creek Golf Course conversion hearings and approval timeline
Monitor implementation of General Plan zoning changes — which areas are being rezoned first
Research Irvine Company's pricing strategy for new developments — are they adding supply or managing scarcity
Track November 2026 Irvine council candidates' positions on General Plan implementation
Coordinate with People for Housing OC on monitoring implementation across OC
Support pro-housing candidates for Irvine mayor and council seats
Publicize General Plan adoption as a model for other OC cities considering zoning capacity increases

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Irvine General Plan — Implementation & Irvine Company Monitoring often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

active_campaigns.title: RHNA Compliance Tracking Across OC
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 20, 2026): Voice of OC reports (April 2026) that many Orange County cities are failing at low-income housing development despite RHNA compliance on paper — the gap between zoned capacity and actual construction is widest in the very low and low-income categories. This confirms the campaign's thesis: compliance without construction is performative.

⚡ STATE CONTEXT (April 14): OC Board of Supervisors backed the proposed $10B state Affordable Housing Bond for the 2026 ballot, plus Local Housing Trust Fund and CA Housing Accelerator funding eligibility. If voters approve the bond, it creates a major new funding stream for affordable units in OC.

Build and maintain a public dashboard tracking all 35 Orange County cities' compliance with their Regional Housing Needs Assessment (RHNA) targets. California law assigns each city a specific number of housing units it must zone for across four income categories. Most OC cities are behind on their targets, especially for very low and low-income units. Making this data publicly accessible and understandable creates accountability pressure and identifies which cities need the most advocacy attention.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: What gets measured gets managed. A public, regularly updated compliance dashboard turns abstract state mandates into concrete, city-by-city accountability. Media can use it for stories. Advocates can prioritize cities with the worst compliance. Residents can see how their city compares. This is the infrastructure that makes all other housing advocacy more effective.
next_actions: Compile RHNA allocation data for all 35 OC cities from SCAG (Southern California Association of Governments)
Research each city's Housing Element status and permitted unit counts
Design dashboard format — city-by-city comparison with compliance percentage by income category
Identify data sources for ongoing tracking (HCD reports, city annual progress reports)
Partner with Kennedy Commission on data sharing and methodology
Launch beta dashboard and solicit feedback from partner organizations

active_campaigns.title: Builder's Remedy Awareness & Support
active_campaigns.summary: Many OC residents, developers, and even city officials don't fully understand Builder's Remedy — the state law provision that allows projects with affordable units to bypass local zoning in cities with non-compliant Housing Elements. We educate residents about what it means, support developers who file Builder's Remedy applications, and counter misinformation from NIMBYs who frame it as a threat rather than a consequence of noncompliance.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Builder's Remedy is powerful but underutilized because developers face political risk and community opposition. Organized community support for Builder's Remedy projects changes the political dynamic — developers know they're not alone, councils know there's constituency for the projects, and residents understand that the 'threat' is actually a consequence of their city's own noncompliance. Education reduces opposition; organized support enables development.
next_actions: Create explainer content: What is Builder's Remedy? Why does it apply in your city?
Map all OC cities currently in Builder's Remedy status
Document pending Builder's Remedy applications across OC
Develop template public comment supporting Builder's Remedy projects
Coordinate with developers filing Builder's Remedy applications to provide community support
Counter misinformation campaigns by city officials who frame Builder's Remedy as an attack on local control

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Builder's Remedy Awareness & Support often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

aligned_organizations.name: People for Housing OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Core local ally — strongest YIMBY voice in OC with only 2 staffers. We amplify their work and provide volunteer capacity.

aligned_organizations.name: Kennedy Commission
aligned_organizations.relationship: Affordable housing research partner. Their data on housing need and income categories strengthens our RHNA compliance advocacy.

aligned_organizations.name: California YIMBY
aligned_organizations.relationship: State-level ally providing legal expertise on housing mandates, Builder's Remedy, and RHNA compliance. They connect us to the statewide movement.

aligned_organizations.name: Irvine Community Land Trust
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on permanently affordable housing goals. Their community land trust model provides an alternative development path.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin YIMBY Action
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-location ally. Same pro-housing mission in a different political context. Austin pushes for policy adoption; we push for policy compliance. Share strategy and lessons.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Every housing unit built is a family formation enabler. Our housing supply advocacy strengthens their family-sized housing campaign.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, Voice of OC coverage, RHNA compliance dashboard as shareable content. Explainer content on Builder's Remedy and state housing mandates.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a Housing Element public hearing or read our city-by-city compliance tracker. Understand how your city compares on RHNA targets.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment supporting housing at your city council. Show up at a Builder's Remedy project hearing. Contact your council member about Housing Element compliance. Share compliance data on social media.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the RHNA tracking research team. Lead advocacy at a specific city council. Coordinate with California YIMBY on state-level compliance pressure. Recruit volunteers for hearing attendance.

immediate.action: Research City of Orange Housing Element update status and timeline
Connect with People for Housing OC for coordination
Map all OC cities currently in Builder's Remedy or non-compliant status
Contact California YIMBY for state-level compliance data and partnership

medium_term.action: Launch RHNA compliance tracker v1 for all 35 OC cities
Organize hearing attendance for Orange Heights Builder's Remedy project
Publish Builder's Remedy explainer and share with OC media
Track Irvine General Plan public hearing schedule and prepare advocacy materials

strategic.action: Build multi-org coalition for RHNA enforcement across OC — People for Housing OC, Kennedy Commission, California YIMBY, labor groups
strategic.notes: Combined advocacy amplifies pressure on non-compliant cities. Coalition letters carry more weight than individual org voices.

strategic.action: Evaluate November 2026 City of Orange council candidates on housing positions
strategic.notes: 3 council seats + mayor on ballot. Coordinate with OC Purple Accountability on candidate questionnaires and scorecards.

strategic.action: File HCD complaints against the most egregiously non-compliant OC cities
strategic.notes: Legal pressure backstops advocacy pressure. Document noncompliance patterns and coordinate with California YIMBY on complaint strategy.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Residents worry that aggressive housing targets can overload roads, schools, and utilities, and they question whether new approvals will deliver truly affordable homes instead of market-rate units beyond reach for local workers.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Plan growth with enforceable infrastructure sequencing and transparent benchmarks.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Housing element compliance frameworks can pair unit pipelines with capital planning and phased implementation.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Accountability means not just approving units on paper, but matching growth with roads, schools, and utilities."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: More legal housing options reduce bidding wars and displacement pressure.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Regions that add supply across price points typically see slower rent growth than constrained markets.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "When every neighborhood adds homes, fewer families get pushed farther away each year."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Follow state law consistently to avoid costly litigation and uncertainty.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Noncompliance with housing mandates can trigger legal risk, project delays, and loss of local control.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Even if you dislike Sacramento mandates, predictable local compliance protects taxpayers and local decision-making."

common_ground: Teachers, nurses, and working families should be able to live in Orange County.
Cities should keep commitments they adopt in official plans.
Growth should be transparent, lawful, and infrastructure-aware.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Avoid framing all homeowner concerns as selfishness.
Avoid promising that RHNA compliance alone solves affordability.
Avoid city-vs-city blame that undermines countywide solutions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Exact pace of project delivery after entitlement approvals.
Interest-rate effects on actual construction starts.
School enrollment and traffic impacts by micro-area.
honest_limits: We cannot compel private builders to start projects on our timeline.
We cannot replace city planning departments or elected councils.
We cannot guarantee deed-restricted affordability outcomes without funding partnerships.
